title: The Beginning of the End

jokes.text: We're in bed by 10:00 most nights. / Well, that's ok. / I can see that. / You just said that you Don't stay up past 10:00. So I didn't know how You had seen Jon Stewart.
jokes.setup: Doctor claims to like the new opening and theme, Larry reveals his insecurities about Jon Stewart guest hosting
jokes.punchline: Doctor catches Larry in a contradiction - he goes to bed at 10 but claims to have seen Jon Stewart's late night guest hosting
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-driven logic trap that reveals Larry's paranoid obsession with Stewart. The therapist's clinical precision makes the contradiction devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: We're in bed by 10:00 most nights.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Well, that's ok. / I can see that.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: You just said that you Don't stay up past 10:00. So I didn't know how You had seen Jon Stewart.

jokes.text: How goes The head doctor? / What's not to like? I got a lollipop, And he shaved me Before the exam.
jokes.setup: Artie asks about Larry's therapy session
jokes.punchline: Larry describes therapy like a pediatric doctor visit with bizarre medical procedure details
jokes.explanation: Larry's childlike deflection through absurd medical imagery. Economy and character integration are strong.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: How goes The head doctor?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What's not to like? I got a lollipop, And he shaved me Before the exam.

jokes.text: First of all, There should be A law on the book That prevents Network people From using The word 'creative' In their job titles. They see it On their stationery And they start Believing it.
jokes.setup: Network assigned a creative consultant
jokes.punchline: Artie's theory that seeing 'creative' on business cards makes network executives delusional
jokes.explanation: Perfect industry satire from Artie. The 'stationery psychology' angle is both original and brutally accurate to the show's cynical worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: First of all, There should be A law on the book That prevents Network people From using The word 'creative' In their job titles. They see it On their stationery And they start Believing it.

jokes.text: What canyon Comes off melrose? / Bullshit canyon, That's what.
jokes.setup: Stevie claimed he was driving into 'the canyon' off Melrose
jokes.punchline: Artie creates a fictional geographical location based on Stevie's lies
jokes.explanation: Artie's cynical wit at its most economical. Perfect setup-punchline structure with character-specific delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What canyon Comes off melrose?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Bullshit canyon, That's what.

jokes.text: el nino is lifting The skirts el northe. Whoa-ho! Whoa! Hey, look at her ass!
jokes.setup: Stevie calling Larry supposedly from Melrose
jokes.punchline: Agent's fake sophisticated talk immediately followed by crude street harassment
jokes.explanation: Shows Stevie's phony sophistication colliding with his true nature. Good character work but the harassment element feels dated.

dialogue.line: el nino is lifting The skirts el northe. Whoa-ho! Whoa! Hey, look at her ass!

jokes.text: That's the trouble With hollywood, Larry. Everyone's got two pricks And three assholes. It's a genitalia Horn of plenty.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Hollywood personalities and Larry's agent
jokes.punchline: Artie's mathematical analysis of Hollywood anatomy culminating in 'genitalia horn of plenty'
jokes.explanation: Peak Artie - combining crude imagery with unexpectedly poetic phrasing. 'Genitalia horn of plenty' is instantly quotable absurdist poetry.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That's the trouble With hollywood, Larry. Everyone's got two pricks And three assholes. It's a genitalia Horn of plenty.

jokes.text: Why does the handwriting Of most of my fan mail Match the handwriting Of the unabomber? / 'Dear sir, your show Is technically crippling. What's left of humanity? Love, ted k.' / This must be for Hank.
jokes.setup: Larry notices his fan mail looks like Unabomber writing, reads a harsh letter
jokes.punchline: After reading a letter calling the show humanity-crippling, Larry assumes it must be for Hank
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - the misdirection from Larry's self-deprecation to casual cruelty toward Hank is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Why does the handwriting Of most of my fan mail Match the handwriting Of the unabomber?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 'Dear sir, your show Is technically crippling. What's left of humanity? Love, ted k.'

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This must be for Hank.

jokes.text: 'Dear sir, your show Is technically crippling. What's left of humanity? Love, ted k.' This must be for Hank.
jokes.setup: Reading fake fan mail supposedly from Ted Kaczynski
jokes.punchline: Assumes the harsh criticism must be intended for Hank
jokes.explanation: Good callback that also shows Larry's ego - can't imagine harsh criticism being about him. Character-driven deflection.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Dear sir, your show Is technically crippling. What's left of humanity? Love, ted k.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This must be for Hank.

jokes.text: What are they Doing up so late? / Who gives a shit? They're watching you.
jokes.setup: Kenny mentions they're up in children 2 to 9 demographic
jokes.punchline: Kenny's callous dismissal of child welfare concerns in favor of ratings
jokes.explanation: Kenny's soulless network executive mentality perfectly captured in his amoral response to Larry's reasonable concern.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What are they Doing up so late?

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Who gives a shit? They're watching you.

jokes.text: Let's all join The fucking circus.
jokes.setup: Kenny excitedly mentions the network is doing cartwheels over ratings
jokes.punchline: Artie's dry suggestion they literally join the circus
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy from Artie - five words that deflate Kenny's entire enthusiastic speech.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Let's all join The fucking circus.

jokes.text: We're beating Letterman In indianapolis. His own backyard.
jokes.setup: Kenny excited about beating Letterman somewhere
jokes.punchline: It's only in Indianapolis, which Kenny calls Letterman's 'backyard'
jokes.explanation: Shows how Kenny inflates minor victories. The geographic specificity makes the small victory even smaller.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: We're beating Letterman In indianapolis. His own backyard.

jokes.text: This desk is the most Lasting relationship That Larry's ever had. He's married To this desk. It's there for him Every night, And it allows him To come in from the rear.
jokes.setup: Kenny wants to try the show without Larry's desk
jokes.punchline: Artie compares Larry's relationship with his desk to marriage, ending with sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Artie's protective instincts manifest as psychological analysis with a crude twist. The escalation from emotional to sexual is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: This desk is the most Lasting relationship That Larry's ever had. He's married To this desk. It's there for him Every night, And it allows him To come in from the rear.

jokes.text: The guy From men at work? / [Sighs] You're killing us. / That's 3 guys. Where's the sex?
jokes.setup: Colin Hay is mentioned as a guest
jokes.punchline: Kenny's horror at booking someone from an '80s band, Artie's frustrated sigh and complaint about lack of sex appeal
jokes.explanation: Standard show business desperation comedy. The characters' reactions are in character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: The guy From men at work?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: [Sighs] You're killing us. / That's 3 guys. Where's the sex?

jokes.text: We'll have our bookers Scour sunset after midnight, And the first celeb We find getting blown By a hooker, Consider him booked. Or her.
jokes.setup: Kenny wants Hugh Grant-style scandal to attract viewers
jokes.punchline: Artie's plan to literally hunt for celebrities in compromising positions, with casual gender inclusivity
jokes.explanation: Artie's morally bankrupt but practical solution, with the 'or her' addition showing his thorough depravity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: We'll have our bookers Scour sunset after midnight, And the first celeb We find getting blown By a hooker, Consider him booked. Or her.

jokes.text: I thought we sprayed For roaches last week.
jokes.setup: Kenny has been talking and making changes
jokes.punchline: Artie compares Kenny to vermin that should have been exterminated
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie economy - one line that completely dehumanizes Kenny while referencing routine pest control.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I thought we sprayed For roaches last week.

jokes.text: Uh, here? / Further. / Can't, uh, Go any further. / Right here, Hank. / Yeah, it's, uh-- It's fresher.
jokes.setup: Kenny is moving Hank to a new position near the band
jokes.punchline: Hank is literally moved to the edge of the stage/set, as far as physically possible from Larry, with Kenny's absurd 'fresher' justification
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy showing Hank's marginalization. The visual of him being pushed to the literal edge while Kenny calls it 'fresh' is perfectly cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Uh, here?

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Further.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Can't, uh, Go any further.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Right here, Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's, uh-- It's fresher.

jokes.text: Can I share Something with you? Um, I have a severe Hearing disorder. Actually, It's a, uh... Punctured eardrum, And, uh, Whenever I'm, uh... Very near A loud noise Such as a... A band, My head feels Like it's just... [Groans] I mean, Have you seen The zapruder film?
jokes.setup: Hank is positioned uncomfortably close to the band
jokes.punchline: Hank's elaborate medical excuse escalating to comparing his head pain to JFK's assassination
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - his desperate medical excuses escalate to historically inappropriate comparisons. The Zapruder film reference is brilliant character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Can I share Something with you? Um, I have a severe Hearing disorder. Actually, It's a, uh... Punctured eardrum, And, uh, Whenever I'm, uh... Very near A loud noise Such as a... A band, My head feels Like it's just... I mean, Have you seen The zapruder film?

jokes.text: Can't, uh, go any further.
jokes.setup: Kenny tells him to move further back
jokes.punchline: Hank hits the back wall, literally can't go further
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the visual gag. Hank's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Can't, uh, go any further.

jokes.text: This is just A stepping stone to Prime time for me, A tired little Piece-of-shit Stepping stone.
jokes.setup: Kenny and Artie arguing about the show's future
jokes.punchline: Kenny reveals his contempt for the show he's supposed to be improving
jokes.explanation: Kenny's mask slips to reveal his true feelings. The contradiction between 'stepping stone' and 'piece-of-shit' shows his ambitious callousness.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: This is just A stepping stone to Prime time for me, A tired little Piece-of-shit Stepping stone.

jokes.text: How many guys are Gonna come in here And blow me?
jokes.setup: Multiple people giving Larry bad news about his contract
jokes.punchline: Larry uses sexual metaphor for being screwed over, delivered in inappropriate setting
jokes.explanation: Shows Larry's frustration boiling over into inappropriate language. Character-appropriate but somewhat crude.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How many guys are Gonna come in here And blow me?

jokes.text: We're on the top floor. Someone on The fucking roof?
jokes.setup: Kenny says pressure is coming from 'upstairs'
jokes.punchline: Artie's literal interpretation leads to the absurd image of executives on the roof
jokes.explanation: Artie's literal-minded logic cuts through corporate speak to create an absurd image. Perfect economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: We're on the top floor. Someone on The fucking roof?

jokes.text: How many guys are Gonna come in here And blow me? / Do I look deaf to you? 'Cause you're talking To me like I'm deaf. Do deaf people walk around In $3,000 suits? I don't think so!
jokes.setup: Multiple network people are delivering bad news to Stevie
jokes.punchline: Stevie's crude question followed by his bizarre logic about deaf people and expensive suits
jokes.explanation: Peak Stevie - combining crude metaphors with completely illogical fashion-based arguments. His manic energy drives the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: How many guys are Gonna come in here And blow me?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Do I look deaf to you? 'Cause you're talking To me like I'm deaf. Do deaf people walk around In $3,000 suits? I don't think so!

jokes.text: You guys can just Take your cocks, And you can Put them on at 11:30 With all The other cocks, And you can call it The cock network.
jokes.setup: Stevie is angry about Larry's treatment
jokes.punchline: Stevie's crude anatomical metaphor for competing shows and networks
jokes.explanation: Stevie's vulgar imagination creates a metaphorical TV schedule. In character but somewhat juvenile.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You guys can just Take your cocks, And you can Put them on at 11:30 With all The other cocks, And you can call it The cock network.

jokes.text: Yeah. They Took the veal Off the menu. I hate This fucking place.
jokes.setup: Agent asks about opportunity at the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Jon interprets 'opportunity' as menu changes, not career advancement
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection showing Jon's discomfort with the career machinations around him. The mundane concern masks deeper anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Jon Stewart
dialogue.line: Yeah. They Took the veal Off the menu. I hate This fucking place.

jokes.text: Jesus christ, my rectal tract Paid for your jaguar.
jokes.setup: Hank is asking his doctor for a fake medical note
jokes.punchline: Hank's crude reminder of how much money he's paid in medical bills
jokes.explanation: Hank's desperate vulgarity while trying to leverage his medical relationship. The specificity of 'rectal tract' makes it perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Jesus christ, my rectal tract Paid for your jaguar.

jokes.text: This move to the band Is like getting on The fucking bus To auschwitz. I'm on my way out.
jokes.setup: Hank realizes his seating change is part of being pushed out
jokes.punchline: Hank compares his career situation to Holocaust transportation
jokes.explanation: Hank's typical melodramatic overreach. While in character, the Holocaust comparison crosses into inappropriateness without sufficient comedic payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: This move to the band Is like getting on The fucking bus To auschwitz. I'm on my way out.

jokes.text: I thought you were 32. / Yeah. Well...
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions being in Entertainment Weekly's under-30 list
jokes.punchline: Larry catches Stevie lying about his age, Stevie's weak deflection
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment showing Stevie's vanity and dishonesty. Larry's deadpan delivery sells the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I thought you were 32.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah. Well...

jokes.text: I had to eat lunch, Larry. / With Jon Stewart. Yeah. / [Band starts playing] I see.
jokes.setup: Stevie explains why he represents Jon Stewart now
jokes.punchline: Larry's devastated 'I see' followed by band music starting, perfect comedic timing
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe timing - Larry's world collapses in two words, then the band kicks in. The structure is flawless, showing Larry's isolation.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I had to eat lunch, Larry. / With Jon Stewart. Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I see.

jokes.text: I remember you Tooting up behind a tree, Using your cell phone.
jokes.setup: Stevie claims he was at Larry's mother's funeral to show loyalty
jokes.punchline: Artie remembers Stevie doing cocaine and business calls during the funeral
jokes.explanation: Artie's elephant memory for Stevie's inappropriate behavior. The specific image of drug use at a funeral is perfectly damning.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I remember you Tooting up behind a tree, Using your cell phone.

jokes.text: Well, we're all A little nervous here At the Larry Sanders show Because the network Is requiring us To attend a sexual Harassment seminar On friday, And I still Don't have a date.
jokes.setup: Larry mentions mandatory sexual harassment training
jokes.punchline: Larry treats the seminar as a dating opportunity, missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry obliviousness - turning workplace compliance into personal opportunity. The irony is perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, we're all A little nervous here At the Larry Sanders show Because the network Is requiring us To attend a sexual Harassment seminar On friday, And I still Don't have a date.

jokes.text: That's what makes it A great company. / Country. / What did I say?
jokes.setup: Stevie making a patriotic speech about America
jokes.punchline: Stevie accidentally calls America a 'company' instead of country, then doesn't understand the correction
jokes.explanation: Stevie's Hollywood mindset where everything is business. His confusion at the correction shows how deeply corporate his thinking is.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's what makes it A great company.
Country.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What did I say?

jokes.text: The chicken flu Is really something. Boy, oh, boy, My chicken has been In bed for 6 weeks. I really just hope My cock doesn't get it.
jokes.setup: Larry discusses chicken flu in his monologue
jokes.punchline: Double entendre about his 'cock' (rooster) potentially getting sick
jokes.explanation: Standard late-night TV double entendre. Functional but unoriginal - exactly the kind of safe material Larry would do on his show.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The chicken flu Is really something. Boy, oh, boy, My chicken has been In bed for 6 weeks. I really just hope My cock doesn't get it.

jokes.text: I got a lot of letters Asking if, uh... You know, there's any way The people at home Can see my lap Throughout The whole show, And, uh, here it is. You got A clear shot, uh... Honestly, that desk Was totally, uh... In the way.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to justify the new no-desk format to the audience
jokes.punchline: Larry's awkward claim that viewers wanted to see his lap, his obvious discomfort with the change
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry discomfort - trying to sell network changes with increasingly ridiculous justifications. His awkwardness drives the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I got a lot of letters Asking if, uh... You know, there's any way The people at home Can see my lap Throughout The whole show, And, uh, here it is. You got A clear shot, uh... Honestly, that desk Was totally, uh... In the way.

jokes.text: Do you like The new set, Hank? [Audience laughs] Oh, that's right. So Hank is over By the, uh, band now
jokes.setup: Larry asks Hank a question, forgetting he's been moved
jokes.punchline: Larry realizes Hank is too far away to respond properly, audience laughs at the awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Hank's marginalization. Larry's confusion and the audience's awareness create layered comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Do you like The new set, Hank?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's right. So Hank is over By the, uh, band now

jokes.text: I'm sorry. What? / Are you-- Are you comfortable? / You know, This is, uh... Are you, uh-- Do we have a beeper For Hank?
jokes.setup: Communication breakdown between Larry and Hank continues
jokes.punchline: Larry's desperate suggestion of a beeper to communicate with Hank
jokes.explanation: The communication breakdown escalates to absurd technological solutions. Larry's increasing desperation is perfectly played.

dialogue.line: I'm sorry. What?

dialogue.line: Are you-- Are you comfortable?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know, This is, uh... Are you, uh-- Do we have a beeper For Hank?

jokes.text: I think It's beautiful. I think it's, uh... Downtown. [Audience laughs] Which city downtown In which, you know? Which city?
jokes.setup: Hank trying to compliment the new set from far away
jokes.punchline: Hank's nonsensical description of the set as 'downtown,' Larry's confused follow-up
jokes.explanation: Pure Hank absurdity - 'downtown' as a set description makes no sense but sounds vaguely sophisticated. His confusion is infectious.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I think It's beautiful. I think it's, uh... Downtown.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Which city downtown In which, you know? Which city?

jokes.text: How do you like The big, comfy chairs? / They're airy. Very airy.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to make conversation with guest Colin Hay during his performance
jokes.punchline: Colin's minimal, awkward responses about the chairs being 'airy'
jokes.explanation: Awkward late-night TV small talk. Colin's minimal responses highlight Larry's desperation to fill airtime.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How do you like The big, comfy chairs?

dialogue.speaker: Colin Hay
dialogue.line: They're airy. Very airy.

jokes.text: Well, maybe we Ought to get you One of those long pillows Shaped like a woman, so, You know, If you get nervous, You can start Humping one of them.
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on the airy, comfortable chairs
jokes.punchline: Larry's inappropriate suggestion about body pillows and public masturbation
jokes.explanation: Larry's inappropriate tangent shows his discomfort with the new format. The suggestion is crude without being particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Larry

jokes.explanation: Peak Larry social awkwardness - turning an innocent comment into personal physical criticism. The escalation is beautifully uncomfortable.

dialogue.line: Have one?

dialogue.line: No! It's not that at all, But that's a very Interesting thing You mentioned there, That I do Have a lazy eye.

jokes.text: Basically, I can look at you, And this eye, If it gets bored Or if somebody's Talking to me, This eye just goes, 'Ah'-- Just looks over here, You know. / I'm sorry. This gets very-- / I think it's a gift. I wish I could Look away.
jokes.setup: Colin explains his lazy eye condition in detail
jokes.punchline: Larry's inappropriate response that he wishes he could look away too
jokes.explanation: Masterful cringe escalation - Colin's vulnerable explanation met with Larry's tone-deaf 'I wish I could look away.' The honesty makes it devastating.

dialogue.line: Basically, I can look at you, And this eye, If it gets bored Or if somebody's Talking to me, This eye just goes, 'Ah'-- Just looks over here, You know.

dialogue.line: I'm sorry. This gets very--

jokes.text: Look how Uncomfortable I am. / Yeah, well, it's Gonna take some Getting used to. / You know, I used to Have a desk myself. I don't know Who I am anymore.
jokes.setup: Larry acknowledging his visible discomfort during the interview
jokes.punchline: Larry's existential crisis over losing his desk, confiding in a guest
jokes.explanation: Perfect vulnerability - Larry breaking the fourth wall to confess his identity crisis to a confused guest. Meta-comedy at its finest.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Look how Uncomfortable I am.

dialogue.speaker: Colin Hay
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it's Gonna take some Getting used to.

dialogue.speaker: Colin Hay

dialogue.line: I don't know Who I am anymore.

jokes.text: Larry, I wrote A screenplay That I think Would be fantastic For patrick swayze. / Great. / Is there any way You could help me Get it to him? / Um... Is my time just about up?
jokes.setup: Colin pitches his Patrick Swayze screenplay to Larry on air
jokes.punchline: Larry's desperate attempt to end the segment immediately
jokes.explanation: Perfect talk show awkwardness - guest using airtime for personal favor, Larry's panic about time. The structure is flawlessly uncomfortable.

dialogue.line: Larry, I wrote A screenplay That I think Would be fantastic For patrick swayze.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Great.

dialogue.speaker: Colin Hay
dialogue.line: Is there any way You could help me Get it to him?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Um... Is my time just about up?

jokes.text: Larry, this is Coming from upstairs. / How could it Be coming From upstairs? I just asked you. / [Whispering] Oh. I get it.
jokes.setup: Kenny refuses Larry's request for his desk back, citing 'upstairs'
jokes.punchline: Larry realizes Kenny is lying - he's the source of the resistance, not higher-ups
jokes.explanation: Brilliant realization moment - Larry sees through Kenny's corporate double-speak. The whispered 'Oh. I get it' is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Larry, this is Coming from upstairs.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How could it Be coming From upstairs? I just asked you.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: [Whispering] Oh. I get it.

jokes.text: Uh, as you may have heard, My contract is up In, uh, 8 weeks, And, uh, I want you To know first That when My contract is up, I will not be coming back.
jokes.setup: Larry making an announcement to his TV audience
jokes.punchline: Larry's impulsive decision to quit on the air, shocking everyone
jokes.explanation: Major character moment - Larry's ultimate assertion of control. The on-air resignation is both shocking and perfectly in character for his impulsiveness.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Uh, as you may have heard, My contract is up In, uh, 8 weeks, And, uh, I want you To know first That when My contract is up, I will not be coming back.

jokes.text: What the fuck Was that? / Shit, I swear I had No idea about this. / Shit! Fuck! Fuck!
jokes.setup: Larry's surprise on-air resignation
jokes.punchline: Immediate backstage panic and Kenny's increasing profanity
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction comedy showing the chaos Larry's decision creates. Kenny's escalating swearing shows his panic about his career.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: What the fuck Was that?

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Shit, I swear I had No idea about this.

dialogue.speaker: Kenny
dialogue.line: Shit! Fuck! Fuck!

jokes.text: You're fired.
jokes.setup: Kenny trying to maintain control after Larry's resignation
jokes.punchline: Larry's simple, final assertion of authority by firing Kenny
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - two words that completely reverse the power dynamic. Larry's ultimate 'put your foot down' moment delivered with perfect understatement.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You're fired.

jokes.text: You're fired.
jokes.setup: Kenny asking Larry about keeping the desk
jokes.punchline: Larry fires him with characteristic bluntness after announcement
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character integration. Two words that perfectly capture Larry finally taking control. The timing after the desk question is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You're fired.

jokes.text: When I said Put your foot down, I think you Misunderstood me.
jokes.setup: Artie advised Larry to 'put your foot down' earlier
jokes.punchline: Artie's dry observation that Larry took his advice too far
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and episode ender. Artie's understated reaction to Larry's nuclear option is quintessential dry comedy. Great structural payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: When I said Put your foot down, I think you Misunderstood me.

tmdb_overview: Kenny Mitchell joins the show as creative consultant and makes major changes to the format. Larry battles with the network and his agent over his contract.
